part: A Sheltered Childhood in Terrell County
title: Born in Terrell County
summary: Browner was born in 1946 in Terrell County, Georgia, to Clifford and Johnny Lou Browner, recounting the story of how his parents met at school after his mother prayed for someone to love her, and how his father left sharecropping for a job at a fertilizer plant in Albany.

title: A Father's Protection From Jim Crow
summary: He describes a father who shielded the children from segregation, keeping them home and looked after rather than working out where he could not watch them, so that growing up in an all-Black community of dirt streets and three-room houses, Browner never had the direct confrontations others did.

title: Dunbar Elementary and the Coal Stove
summary: Browner recalls Dunbar Elementary, a faded former-white building heated by a potbelly stove the children fed with coal they dug from the crawl space, with outdoor toilets and no running water, and the bus that carried him seven miles to Carver High, and a mother who cooked in the white school and then the new Black one.

title: Mass Meetings at Mount Olive
summary: With his father and older sister already involved, Browner came to the movement through the mass meetings moved to his home church, Mount Olive Baptist in Sasser, exciting gatherings where he first saw Black and white intermingling, scriptures and singing mixed with reports of beatings, arrests, and the voter-registration drives across Terrell and Lee counties.

title: A Letter for New Books
summary: He recounts how he and Clovis Jones, the only two of five to sign, wrote the superintendent asking for new books instead of the marked-up hand-me-downs from the white school, and were called to the all-Black school's principal, threatened with expulsion, and treated as troublemakers afterward.

title: The Night the Church Burned
summary: Browner describes being forced to resign as senior class president over the letter, the night Sheriff Z.T. Matthews and armed white men walked through a mass meeting intimidating them, and waking to find Mount Olive burned to the ground, sitting before the smoldering ruins wondering who would burn a church.

title: Fear for the Family
summary: He recalls the hurt in his father and the community, helping deacons rebuild, the rumors that their house was spared only because uninvolved elders lived nearby, and the white woman who tried to run his father off the road, after which the family grew more vigilant and the children more restricted.

title: Joining the Army, and the March on Washington
summary: Unable to afford college, Browner joined the army to save for it, and recounts that the rebuilt church gave him his first hourly wage and the spending money to take his first train ride to the 1963 March on Washington, arriving without a jacket in the unexpected cold.

title: A Day on the Mall
summary: He describes a day mostly walking and listening among more people than he had ever seen, hearing the bands, Peter, Paul and Mary, John Lewis, and Dr. King's speech, with no idea of the day's importance, and recalls King having come to his own church before.

title: Basic Training and Germany
summary: Browner recounts basic training that he found rigorous but fun and that redefined his timid personality, communications and armor training, and twenty months in Kitzingen, Germany, where older people looked after the young soldier and his letters home asked mostly for cake and pecans.

title: Changed by the World
summary: Returning after two and a half years, Browner found the economics of home little changed but himself transformed, wanting more out of life, introduced to jazz and to men from other cities, which convinced him he needed an education to reach what he now wanted.

title: Albany State, Working Two Jobs
summary: He describes choosing to come home and enroll at Albany State rather than wait or follow relatives to Detroit and Flint, majoring in sociology while toying with law school, and working two part-time jobs so hard that he missed nearly every homecoming and basketball game.

title: Reidsville, Flint, and a Job in Five Hours
summary: Browner recounts being the first Black counselor at Reidsville State Prison until a warden forced him out, driving to Flint with eighty dollars after his friend Doug urged him north, the General Motors plant and the computer room he quit over the gossip, and being hired to sell insurance five hours after walking off the job.

title: Twenty Years at General Motors
summary: He describes transferring back to the General Motors plant in Doraville near Atlanta, where he worked twenty years until a collision tied him up, living in DeKalb County and coming home to Terrell County every chance he got.

title: What Changed and What Didn't
summary: Back home for good, Browner finds the economics still depressed, sharecroppers replaced by business farmers who ran Black families off the land for trying to register, a few Black officials but, in his brother's words, little that matters about who drives the wagon when it always goes the same place.

title: The Difference Between North and South
summary: He closes on the surface gains that hide how little changed behind the scenes, and his parable of North and South: in the North a white host might break the plate he used, while in the South he already knows where he stands, a perceived freedom against an honest one, and an economic separation he says has replaced the racial line.
